questions.prompt: Cron job runs daily cleanup. Why make it idempotent?

choices.text: Cloudflare may retry scheduled handlers under certain failure conditions; rare double-runs can happen. If the job is idempotent (deleting where condition met, upserting, checking a 'done' flag), running twice is harmless. If not idempotent (incrementing counters, sending emails, creating records), double-runs cause real damage — duplicate notifications sent to users, double-billed customers, doubled metrics. Pattern: write a flag at job completion (`cleanup:2026-05-20: 'done'`); check the flag at start; skip if already ran today. Or structure operations to be naturally idempotent. Same principle as Lambda invocation idempotency — at-least-once delivery semantics require the consumer to handle duplicates.

questions.explanation: Cron may retry under failures; double-runs are possible. Idempotent jobs handle this safely.

questions.prompt: Need to send 100k personalized emails daily. One cron Worker iterating all users hits CPU limit. Approach?

choices.text: Cron + Queues split. The daily cron fires; enqueues 100k jobs to a Cloudflare Queue (one job per user). A queue consumer Worker processes jobs in batches — Cloudflare automatically scales consumers and bounds concurrency. Each job is small (one email send), well within CPU limits; if any fails, the queue retries that individual job. Cleaner than fitting 100k operations into one cron run. Pattern: cron is the orchestrator (decides what work needs doing); queue is the throughput layer (processes the work). Standard architecture for any 'do something for many entities on a schedule' pattern at scale.

questions.explanation: Cron enqueues jobs to a Queue; queue consumer processes in batches with auto-scaling.
